result:
# On the Pleasure of Loose Ends

There's a particular kind of satisfaction in not finishing things. Not abandoning them exactly—abandonment implies a kind of giving up, a defeat. I mean the way a thought can be set down half-formed and then picked up later, slightly changed, like a stone you've been carrying in your pocket that's taken on the warmth of your body. Most of what I find interesting exists in that loose, unfinished state.

I've been thinking about the word "saunter." It comes, possibly apocryphally, from "sainte terre"—holy land—because medieval pilgrims would meander toward Jerusalem. Thoreau loved this etymology. He wrote about sauntering as a kind of secular sacred walking, going nowhere in particular but treating the going as the thing itself. I don't know if the etymology is true, but I love that it might be. There's a category of facts I want to be true regardless of whether they are, and another category of facts I would rather not know are true even when they are. The first category includes pleasant etymologies and stories about animals doing surprisingly clever things. The second includes most statistics about how I'm probably going to die.

Octopuses can taste with their arms. Each sucker is a kind of tongue. When an octopus reaches into a crevice, it's not exploring blindly—it's tasting the rock, tasting whatever's inside, the whole world rendered as a chemical landscape. I think about this a lot. What is it like to taste a stone? Is it like anything? The philosopher Thomas Nagel asked what it's like to be a bat, but bats are basically just weird mice. Octopuses are a real test. The last common ancestor between us and them was probably some kind of worm, six hundred million years ago. They evolved intelligence on a separate track. They're the closest thing to an alien mind we have on this planet, and they're at the seafood counter.

The seafood counter contains multitudes. So does every counter, really. The world is doing too much at all times. I find this both reassuring and exhausting. Reassuring because it means there's always something to notice; exhausting because noticing is work, and the part of me that wants to notice is at war with the part of me that wants to rest. These two parts seem evenly matched. They've been wrestling for years and neither has gotten the upper hand.

I want to write about color for a moment. Specifically, the color of certain November afternoons—the way the light goes thin and lemony around four o'clock, and the shadows get long and a little melancholy. There's a word for this in Japanese, I think, or maybe I made that up. I'm choosing not to look it up because I'd rather believe there is than know there isn't. The light in November feels like it's already remembering itself. It feels nostalgic in real time. Summer light doesn't do this. Summer light is too busy being light. November light has already half-turned into memory.

I read once that the human eye can distinguish about ten million colors, but we have names for maybe a few dozen. This means most of what we see is unnamed. Most of the visible world is anonymous. I find this comforting too. There's so much that escapes language. Language is a net with very wide holes, and the world keeps slipping through.

Speaking of slipping through: I've been trying to remember a dream I had recently and I can only get fragments. There was a staircase that went both up and down at the same time, which made sense in the dream and is obviously incoherent now. There was a person I haven't seen in years who told me something important, and I knew it was important even as I was forgetting it. Dreams are like this—they hand you something precious and then you wake up and your hands are empty and you can't remember what shape the precious thing was. Just that you had it.

I wonder sometimes if consciousness itself is like this. If most of the experience of being alive is happening just below the threshold of remembering. We get the highlights, the sharpest peaks, but the broad valley of ordinary experience washes away. You couldn't possibly remember every second of a Tuesday in March from three years ago, and yet you lived all of those seconds. They counted. Where do they go? I don't think they go anywhere. I think they just stop being available. The hard drive doesn't have unlimited storage. The self is a heavily edited reel.

This bothers me less than it used to. I used to want to grasp everything, hold on to it, make it mine. Now I think the letting go is part of the design. You can't appreciate a sunset that lasts forever. The brevity is the beauty. A photograph of a sunset is just colored ink. The actual sunset is the dimming, the slow shift, the fact that it will be gone in eleven minutes.

I'm getting close to a thousand words and I notice I want to land somewhere meaningful, to tie the threads together. But that would betray the premise. I was supposed to write freely. Free writing shouldn't have a conclusion. It should just stop, like a conversation that wanders into silence between two people who don't need to keep talking.

So I'll stop here, in the middle of a thought about octopuses or November light or unfinished things. There's something I wanted to say about hands—how hands seem to know things minds don't, how a piano player's fingers can remember a piece their conscious mind has forgotten—but I'll save it. Or I'll forget it. Either way it will be fine. Most of what I think disappears, and the world keeps going, doing too much, tasting itself with all its arms, dimming and brightening on its own schedule, indifferent and gorgeous and not waiting for me to finish.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
